title: Flash Sale — System Design
description: Design a flash-sale / drop system: sell a small, fixed inventory to a huge, simultaneous crowd. The themes are surviving an extreme traffic spike (orders of magnitude over steady state), admitting users fairly, decrementing limited stock without overselling, and degrading gracefully.

nodes.label: Buyer Client
nodes.description: Floods the system at sale start trying to buy limited stock.

nodes.label: CDN / Static Edge
nodes.description: Serves the static product/landing page so reads don't hit the backend.

nodes.label: Edge / API Gateway
nodes.description: First line of admission control: rate-limit, bot-filter, shed load.

nodes.label: Virtual Waiting Room
nodes.description: Admits a fair, capped rate of users into the purchase path.

nodes.label: Admission Token Service
nodes.description: Issues signed, time-boxed tokens that gate the buy endpoint.

nodes.label: Purchase Service
nodes.description: Validates a token and attempts to reserve one unit of stock.

nodes.label: Inventory Counter
nodes.description: Atomic stock counter (e.g. Redis) decremented per successful reserve.

nodes.label: Order Queue
nodes.description: Buffers confirmed reservations for async order creation.

nodes.label: Order Service
nodes.description: Creates the durable order from a reservation.

nodes.label: Order Store
nodes.description: Authoritative orders and the durable stock ledger.

nodes.label: Payment Service
nodes.description: Charges the buyer within the reservation hold window.

functional: Sell a fixed, limited inventory starting at a precise moment.
Let an admitted user buy (typically) one unit.
Create a durable order and charge the buyer for each successful purchase.
nonFunctional: Survive an extreme spike: demand can be 100-1000x the stock at T0.
Never oversell — at most `stock` units are sold.
Fairness: admitted users get a real chance; no unfair advantage to bots.
Graceful degradation: when overloaded, shed load cleanly (clear 'try later'), not crash.
The vast majority will fail to buy — failure must be cheap and fast.

capacity.label: Arrivals at T0
capacity.note: Working example: 10M buyers for 10K units (1000:1). Most must be shed.

capacity.label: Stock
capacity.note: Single SKU; the entire correctness budget is 10K successful reservations.

capacity.label: Admitted rate
capacity.note: Admit ≈ stock / completion_rate (e.g. ~70% pay) ⇒ ~14K admitted for 10K units, metered at a few hundred/sec.

capacity.label: Buy attempts/sec
capacity.note: Derived from the admitted rate, not the 10M arrivals — what the atomic counter must serve.

capacity.label: Waiting-room writes/sec
capacity.note: Enqueue/position writes; must live in the edge tier, never the purchase core.

capacity.label: Counter ops/sec
capacity.note: One Redis key; ~300-1000 DECRs/sec is trivial — the risk is availability, not throughput.

capacity.label: Queue + payment throughput
capacity.note: Order workers and PSP calls run async; scale workers to drain ~14K reservations in minutes.

capacity.label: Hold-window expiry volume
capacity.note: Non-payers (~14K admitted − ~10K paid) expire and return units to the counter.

capacity.label: Sale window
capacity.note: Often sells out almost instantly; steady-state load is tiny — provision for the spike or shed.

api.description: Load the (static, CDN-cached) sale page. Does not touch the backend.

Request admission (authenticated; binds to the caller's account/session). Joins the virtual waiting room and, when admitted, returns a single-use signed token whose claims are { sale_id, user_id, admission_id (nonce/jti), max_qty, exp }. The token is the gate for /buy; expired, forged, or already-consumed tokens are rejected.
api.request: { } (identity from auth header/session; optional bot-challenge result)

api.description: Attempt to buy. Requires a valid, unexpired, unconsumed admission token. Assumes a logged-in buyer with a stored default payment method (a saved PSP customer + payment-method reference) — the buyer picks/confirms it at admission, so /buy carries only a paymentMethodId pointer, never raw card data. The token is consumed single-use atomically with the reservation, so a replayed/shared token cannot reserve twice. A retry with the same idempotencyKey returns the original reservation (no second unit). Reserves one unit if stock remains; the async worker then charges that payment method within the hold window.
Poll the lifecycle of a reservation. Async order/payment work means /buy returns 'reserved' before the charge resolves, so the client polls here to learn the terminal state.

PSP webhook (signature-verified) reporting the outcome of a charge. Idempotent on payment_intent_id so duplicate/replayed webhooks don't double-confirm or double-release. On success the reservation is confirmed and the order created; on failure/timeout it is released.

dataModel.name: inventory_counter (in-memory)
dataModel.note: The hot atomic counter that gates reservations; rebuilt from the ledger on restart.

dataModel.name: waiting_room_entries (edge, sharded)
dataModel.note: Ordered, replicated queue state at the edge; deduped per user so a refresh keeps one place. Drained into admissions at the metered rate.

fields.name: position / enqueued_at
fields.type: bigserial / timestamptz (best-effort FIFO within a window)

fields.name: unique
fields.type: (sale_id, user_id) — one place in line per user per sale

dataModel.name: admissions
dataModel.note: One row per admitted user; backs the signed token and enforces per-user limits.

fields.name: consumed_at
fields.type: timestamptz null (set when the token is spent — single-use)

fields.name: unique
fields.type: (sale_id, user_id) — one admission per user per sale

dataModel.name: reservations
dataModel.note: First-class hold created on a successful counter decrement; the unit of inventory truth.

fields.name: unique
fields.type: (sale_id, idempotency_key) — retries map to the same reservation

dataModel.name: payment_attempts
dataModel.note: Each charge try against the PSP; carries the buyer's stored payment reference and the idempotency key sent to the provider.

dataModel.name: orders
dataModel.note: Durable order created once a reservation is paid.

fields.name: reservation_id
fields.type: uuid FK unique (one order per reservation)

dataModel.name: stock_ledger_events
dataModel.note: Append-only allocation log; the durable source of truth the counter is rebuilt from.

patterns.name: Static edge offload (CDN)
patterns.what: Serve the sale page and read state from the CDN so the backend only sees buy attempts.
patterns.whenToUse: When read traffic dwarfs the actual transactional work.

patterns.name: Admission control / load shedding
patterns.what: Reject or queue most traffic at the edge so the core only handles a survivable rate.
patterns.whenToUse: Demand far exceeds capacity and most requests can't succeed anyway.

patterns.name: Virtual waiting room
patterns.what: Hold users in a fair queue and admit a capped rate into the purchase path.
patterns.whenToUse: Fairness + protecting the core under a massive simultaneous arrival.

patterns.name: Atomic counter reservation
patterns.what: Decrement a single in-memory stock counter atomically; success means you hold a unit.
patterns.whenToUse: Fast, oversell-proof allocation of limited stock under extreme concurrency.

patterns.name: Async order processing
patterns.what: Confirm the reservation instantly, then create the durable order and charge off the hot path.
patterns.whenToUse: Keep the contended path tiny; do durable/slow work behind a queue.

patterns.name: Reconciliation + reaping
patterns.what: Reconcile the fast counter to the durable ledger and release reservations that don't pay in time.
patterns.whenToUse: Fast-counter designs that must stay consistent with durable truth.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Everyone Hits Buy, Decrement Stock in the DB
description: Start with the obvious design: expose a buy endpoint, let every user call it, and on each call read the stock row from the database, check it's above zero, and decrement it. One transaction per purchase, the DB as the single source of truth. For a normal store this is exactly right.

Under a flash sale it melts. Millions of users arrive in the same second for a few thousand units, so the buy endpoint and the database take the full flood — far past what the DB can serve. Worse, 'read then check then decrement' as separate steps lets concurrent buyers all read the same positive count and oversell. Even a single row with `UPDATE ... WHERE qty > 0` becomes a row-lock hotspot that collapses throughput, and most of the traffic is doomed to fail anyway but still costs a full request.
So the naive path motivates the whole design: offload reads at the edge, admit only a survivable, fair rate, and reserve stock with one atomic counter instead of a contended DB row.

view.caption: The buyer client sends a buy request to the Purchase Service, which reads and decrements the stock row in the Order Store.

steps.decisionPrompt: 10 million users hit the buy endpoint in the same second for 10,000 units, each doing a read-check-decrement on one stock row. What breaks first?
whyNow: Letting everyone hit the DB makes the two failures concrete — the spike that melts the database and the race that oversells — which is exactly what edge offload, admission control, and the atomic counter remove.

recap.after: Every buyer calls the endpoint and read-check-decrements the stock row in the DB.
recap.newRisk: The spike overwhelms the DB, separate read/check/decrement steps oversell, and a single hot row becomes a lock bottleneck.

traps.trap: Letting the full crowd transact directly against the database.
traps.why: The spike is orders of magnitude over capacity, and naive read-then-decrement oversells while a hot row serializes throughput.
traps.instead: Offload reads to the edge, admit a fair capped rate, and reserve with a single atomic decrement-if-positive counter.

steps.title: 2. Offload Reads to the Edge
description: Most of the flood is people loading the page and refreshing for availability — not actually transacting. Serve the sale page as static assets from a CDN, and expose availability/sold-out as a cacheable, frequently-updated value the page polls.
This keeps the overwhelming majority of traffic off the backend entirely. The backend should only ever see admission and buy attempts.
Push the sale start as a precise time the client honors, so the spike is a known instant you design for rather than a surprise.

view.caption: The buyer client loads the static page from the CDN / Static Edge and only the 'enter sale' action reaches the Edge / API Gateway.

steps.decisionPrompt: What fraction of flash-sale traffic is actually trying to transact vs just looking?

concepts.definition: Serving pages and read-only state from CDN cache rather than the origin.
concepts.whyItMatters: Absorbs the bulk of the spike before it reaches any backend.
concepts.example: Sold-out flag cached at the edge with a 1s TTL; the page polls the CDN, not the DB.

whyNow: Cutting read load at the edge is the cheapest, highest-leverage move; everything after only has to handle the residual transactional traffic.

recap.before: Every read and buy hits the backend; the DB takes the full flood.
recap.after: Reads absorbed by CDN; backend sees only buy/admission attempts.
recap.newRisk: Even the residual transactional traffic at T0 can swamp the core — need admission control.

traps.trap: Letting the page poll the backend for availability.
traps.why: Millions polling per second is itself a DDoS on your own DB.
traps.instead: Serve availability from the CDN edge with a short TTL.

steps.title: 3. Admission Control at the Edge
description: Demand is 100-1000x stock, so most requests cannot succeed. Reject early and cheaply: rate-limit per user/IP, filter bots, and shed excess load at the edge with a fast, clear 'try later' instead of letting it reach the core.
The goal is to protect the purchase core so it only ever receives a survivable request rate — admitting somewhat more than `stock` (to cover non-completers) but nowhere near the full flood.

Admission is a feedback controller, not a fixed constant. The capacity math (`admit ≈ stock / completion_rate`, metered over 1-5 min) is just the starting set-point. At runtime the admission service watches the live counter value, reservation-success and payment-completion rates, hold-expiry rate, queue backlog, and worker/payment health, and adjusts: it slows or pauses when the counter is near zero, when order/payment workers are unhealthy, or when the counter epoch is uncertain (fail closed), and it admits another tranche when holds expire or payment completion runs lower than expected so the remaining units still find buyers.
Failure here must be the cheap, common case: a shed request should cost almost nothing.

view.caption: The buyer client's 'enter sale' request hits the Edge / API Gateway, which queues it for admission in the Virtual Waiting Room.

steps.decisionPrompt: If 10M users hit at once for 10K units, what should happen to the other 9.99M requests?

concepts.definition: Deliberately rejecting excess requests to keep the system within capacity.
concepts.whyItMatters: A system that sheds cleanly survives; one that tries to serve everyone collapses.
concepts.example: Edge returns 429/try-later once admitted rate hits the cap.

concepts.definition: Treating the admission rate as a controller driven by live outcomes, not a fixed constant set once from capacity math.
concepts.whyItMatters: The right number of admissions depends on how fast reservations convert to paid orders, which only the running sale reveals.
concepts.example: Slow/pause admission when the counter nears zero or workers are unhealthy; admit another tranche when holds expire.

whyNow: Admission control is the core survival mechanism; stating that most traffic must be rejected cheaply is the key senior insight.

recap.before: All transactional traffic reaches the core.
recap.after: Edge sheds the excess; core sees a survivable rate.
recap.newRisk: Raw shedding is unfair (luck of the millisecond) — need fairness.

steps.title: 4. Fairness: Virtual Waiting Room
description: Pure load-shedding is a lottery on network timing and favors bots with fast connections. A virtual waiting room makes admission fair: users join a queue and are admitted at a controlled rate.

Admission grants a signed, time-boxed token that the buy endpoint requires. Its claims bind it to one identity — { sale_id, user_id, admission_id (nonce), max_qty, exp } — so it isn't a bearer ticket anyone can share. Abuse defenses ride on this: enter is authenticated and admissions are unique per (sale_id, user_id), so one account gets one place in line; the token is single-use (consumed atomically at buy), so a replayed or shared token can't reserve twice; and a bot-challenge/risk score can gate entry. Per-user and per-household purchase limits are enforced via max_qty plus the unique reservation constraint.
Communicate position/wait so users don't hammer retry; the waiting room itself must be cheap (often edge/CDN-backed) since everyone passes through it.

view.caption: The Edge / API Gateway queues arrivals in the Virtual Waiting Room, which has the Admission Token Service issue a signed token back to the buyer client.

options.name: FIFO virtual waiting room (default)
options.description: Arrivals are held in a durable ordered queue at the edge and admitted in arrival order behind signed admission tokens, metered to match inventory drain.
pros: Admits buyers in arrival order, which feels fair and is easy to communicate ('you are number 42,001 in line').
Decouples the 10M arrivals from the purchase core by holding them behind signed admission tokens.
A position-based queue lets you show progress and meter admission to match inventory drain.
cons: Requires a durable, ordered queue that itself must absorb 10M concurrent entrants.
Strict ordering rewards whoever's network was fastest at the start gun, not a true lottery.
Queue position must be tracked per user, adding state to the admission tier.

view.caption: The Edge / API Gateway queues arrivals in the FIFO Virtual Waiting Room, which admits them in order via the Admission Token Service's signed token.

options.name: Random lottery admission
options.description: Entrants in a time window are collected into a set, and the waiting room draws admittees at random, sizing each draw to available inventory.
pros: Removes the fastest-network advantage — every entrant in a window has equal odds.
No need to maintain strict global ordering, just a set of entrants per draw.
Naturally smooths the admission rate by sizing each draw to inventory.
cons: Feels arbitrary to users who can't see a position or progress.
Must still collect and dedupe the full entrant set before drawing.
Losers may re-enter, complicating fairness across multiple draws.

view.caption: The Edge / API Gateway feeds entrants into the Lottery Admission, which issues tokens to randomly drawn winners through the Admission Token Service.

options.name: Token-bucket throttle (no ordering)
options.description: The edge admits requests at a fixed rate from a token bucket until inventory is gone and drops the rest, keeping no per-user queue position.
pros: Simplest to operate: admit at a fixed rate (e.g. 500/s) until inventory is gone, drop the rest.
No per-user queue state, so the admission tier stays nearly stateless.
Caps load on the purchase core precisely regardless of arrival burst.
cons: No fairness guarantee — retrying clients with better timing get admitted repeatedly.
Rejected users get a hard 'try again' with no sense of position or chance.
Bursty retries can still hammer the edge even when buckets are empty.

view.caption: The Edge / API Gateway passes arrivals to a Token-Bucket Throttle that admits at a fixed rate via the Admission Token Service, keeping no queue position.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you make admission fair rather than 'whoever's packet arrived first'?

concepts.definition: A queue that admits a capped, fair rate of users into the protected path with a token.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns a stampede into an orderly, throttled, fairer flow and gates the core.
concepts.example: Admit N users/sec with signed tokens; the rest wait with a position.

concepts.definition: A time-boxed, verifiable token required to call the buy endpoint.
concepts.whyItMatters: Caps purchase-path concurrency and blocks un-admitted/bot traffic.
concepts.example: buy requires a valid token; expired/forged tokens are rejected at the edge.

deepDives.title: Durable waiting-room state and recovery
points: At 10M arrivals in 1-2 s the queue state is the first hard production problem — 'durable ordered queue at the edge' has to be specified, not hand-waved. Give each entrant a `queue_entry_id` and enforce a `(sale_id, user_id)` uniqueness so a refreshing client or retry maps back to its existing place instead of taking a second one (and can't be admitted twice).
Shard the queue across many edge partitions (e.g. by region or by hash of user_id) so no single node absorbs 10M writes; admission then drains round-robin across shards at the global set-point rate.

Persist/replicate each shard's ordered log (the same broker class as the order queue, or the edge provider's managed waiting room) so an edge-region failure replays entrants from the log on recovery rather than dropping everyone's place. Admission is idempotent on `queue_entry_id`, so replay can't double-issue tokens.

Be honest about FIFO scope: true global FIFO across 10M sharded entrants is expensive and rarely worth it. Frame it as best-effort FIFO within an arrival window — ordered within a shard/window, approximately ordered across shards — which is fair enough to communicate ('you're ~#42,000') without a global total order.
Managed waiting rooms (Cloudflare/Fastly) give this durability, sharding, and recovery turnkey; self-hosting means building the ordered, replicated, edge-resident queue yourself.

failureDrills.scenario: A reseller spins up 50K fresh accounts before T0 to flood the waiting room and win most of the stock.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Bulk fake accounts shouldn't translate into bulk admissions or purchases; real buyers keep a fair chance.
failureDrills.mitigation: Combine pre-sale account eligibility (account age / prior activity / verification), device + risk scoring and velocity checks at entry, a bot challenge gating token issuance, and per-household / per-payment-method limits beyond the per-account `max_qty` — with a manual override + appeal path for false positives. The waiting room raises the cost of each fake identity rather than promising perfect bot-proofing.

failureDrills.scenario: An admitted user shares or replays their signed admission token across many browser sessions/devices.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: One admission yields at most one reserved unit; extra sessions gain nothing.
failureDrills.mitigation: Tokens are identity-bound (sale_id, user_id, admission_id) and consumed single-use atomically at buy; admissions are unique per (sale_id, user_id) and reservations unique per (sale_id, idempotency_key), so replays collapse to the same reservation.

failureDrills.scenario: The edge region holding part of the waiting room fails mid-sale.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Entrants on that shard don't silently lose their place or get admitted twice on recovery.
failureDrills.mitigation: Replay the shard's replicated ordered log; admission idempotent on queue_entry_id; surviving shards keep draining so the sale doesn't stall.

whyNow: Fairness is an explicit requirement and the differentiator from a generic rate limiter; the token also becomes the gate for the buy path.

recap.after: Fair, throttled admission with tokens gating the core.
recap.newRisk: Admitted users now contend on the single scarce resource: stock.

steps.title: 5. Oversell-Proof Reservation
description: Admitted buyers contend for limited stock. The whole correctness requirement reduces to: at most `stock` reservations succeed. Use a single atomic counter — but be precise about the primitive. A plain `DECR` that goes negative and then needs a compensating `INCR` is racy; use a guarded conditional instead: a Redis Lua script (`if GET > 0 then DECR`), a `WATCH`/`MULTI` transaction, or a DynamoDB conditional write (`UpdateItem` with `ConditionExpression remaining > 0`). The decrement happens only while units remain.
An atomic counter is the contention point but is extremely fast and trivially oversell-proof: the (stock+1)th decrement is rejected by the guard and returns 'sold out'. No locking, no race.

Make idempotency explicit. The buy is keyed by (sale_id, idempotencyKey) with a unique constraint on the `reservations` row, and the admission token is consumed single-use in the same atomic step. So a retry — same key — maps back to the existing reservation and returns it, rather than consuming a second unit or a second token. Success here is a reservation (a durable hold), not yet a paid order.

view.caption: The buyer client's buy goes to the Purchase Service, which verifies the admission token and atomically decrements-if-positive against the Inventory Counter.

options.name: Redis atomic decrement-if-positive (default)
options.description: A single in-memory Redis counter is atomically decremented only while positive, rejecting oversell at memory speed before the slower durable order write.
pros: A single-threaded atomic counter decrement is O(1) and rejects oversell at memory speed.
Handles the 10k-buyers-in-one-millisecond burst without row contention in the database.
Decouples the hot reservation check from the slower durable order write.
cons: The counter is in-memory, so a node crash needs reconciliation against the durable ledger.
A single hot key is a throughput ceiling and must be replicated/failover-ready.
Two sources of truth (counter and ledger) must be reconciled after failures.

view.caption: The Purchase Service verifies the admission token, then atomically decrements-if-positive the in-memory Inventory Counter to reject oversell at memory speed.

options.name: DB row lock / SELECT FOR UPDATE
options.description: Each buy locks the stock-count row in the order database and decrements it within a transaction that also inserts the order, so stock and order commit together.
pros: Single source of truth: the stock count lives in the durable order store, no reconciliation.
Transactional correctness — the decrement and the order insert commit together.
Familiar relational semantics, easy to reason about.
cons: 10k concurrent buyers serialize on one row, collapsing throughput to lock-acquisition rate.
Lock contention causes timeouts and retries that amplify load — exactly the naive failure.
The database becomes the bottleneck the whole design was built to avoid.

view.caption: The Purchase Service verifies the token, then takes a SELECT FOR UPDATE row lock in the Order Store to decrement stock and insert the order together.

options.name: Pre-minted reservation tokens
options.description: Exactly one single-use claim token is minted per unit up front, and holding a token from the pool structurally guarantees a unit, so oversell cannot occur.
pros: Mint exactly one single-use claim token per unit up front; possessing one guarantees a unit.
Oversell is structurally impossible — there are only as many tokens as units.
Token check can be distributed/sharded, removing the single hot counter.
cons: Unredeemed tokens (abandoned carts) strand inventory until they expire and are reclaimed.
Need a TTL + reclaim process to recirculate tokens, adding machinery.
Fairly distributing the finite token pool reintroduces an admission decision.

view.caption: The Purchase Service verifies the token, claims a single-use token from the Reservation Token Pool (100), and enqueues to the Order Queue only if claimed.

steps.decisionPrompt: 10,000 admitted users hit buy at the same millisecond for 100 units. How do you guarantee exactly 100 succeed?

concepts.definition: A single guarded atomic operation that reserves a unit only while the counter is above zero.
concepts.whyItMatters: Oversell-proof under unlimited concurrency with no locks and no compensating increment.
concepts.example: Redis Lua `if GET>0 then DECR`, or DynamoDB UpdateItem with ConditionExpression remaining>0.

concepts.definition: Keying a write so retries produce one effect: the same (sale_id, idempotencyKey) maps to the same reservation.
concepts.whyItMatters: Clients retry aggressively under a spike; without it a retry would consume a second unit.
concepts.example: Unique (sale_id, idempotency_key) on reservations; a duplicate buy returns the original reservationId.

whyNow: The atomic counter is the precise mechanism that makes overselling impossible; collapsing the requirement to one atomic op is the senior move.

recap.before: Stock could be oversold under concurrency.
recap.newRisk: Reservations must become durable orders + payment without re-contending on the hot counter.

traps.trap: Read stock, check > 0, then decrement in separate steps.
traps.why: Concurrent buyers all read the same positive value and oversell.

traps.trap: Decrementing a SQL row per buy under the full spike.
traps.why: Row-lock contention on one row collapses throughput.
traps.instead: An in-memory atomic counter as the hot reservation gate; reconcile to durable storage off the hot path.

flows.name: Token-gated atomic reservation
flows.note: An admitted buyer presents a token; the purchase service verifies it, then a single atomic decrement-if-positive either reserves a unit or returns sold-out — oversell-proof and lock-free.

steps.title: 6. Async Order Creation and Payment
description: Keep the contended buy path tiny: a successful counter decrement returns 'reserved' immediately and enqueues the reservation. Order creation, persistence, and payment happen asynchronously off the hot path.

This assumes the buyer is already a logged-in customer with a stored payment method: /buy passed a paymentMethodId, and the reservation carries the PSP customer + payment-method reference. So the worker can charge without any further buyer interaction — there is no separate 'enter card' step racing the hold window. (A guest-checkout variant would instead create a checkout session at admission and have the buyer complete it within the hold.)

A worker creates the durable order, decrements the durable stock ledger, and charges that stored payment method within a reservation hold window. The slow/durable work is now decoupled from the spike.
If payment fails or times out, the reservation expires and the unit is released back — so a non-paying winner doesn't permanently consume stock.

view.caption: The Purchase Service enqueues the reservation to the Order Queue, and the Order Service consumes it to persist the order and decrement the ledger in the Order Store, then charges via the Payment Service.

options.name: Async order + payment via queue (default)
options.description: The buy request returns the moment the reservation succeeds, then an order service consumes a durable queue to create the order and call the payment gateway off the hot path.
pros: The buy request returns the instant the reservation succeeds, keeping admitted-user latency low.
Order creation and the slow payment call run off a durable queue, absorbing the burst.
A failed charge can retry or release the reservation without blocking the buyer's request.
cons: The buyer is told 'reserved', not 'paid' — the UX must explain the pending state.
Requires an idempotent order pipeline and a path to release stock if payment ultimately fails.
End-to-end completion is eventually consistent, complicating confirmation emails/receipts.

view.caption: After reserving, the Purchase Service enqueues to the Order Queue; the Order Service consumes it off the hot path to persist the order in the Order Store and charge the Payment Service.

options.name: Synchronous order + charge in the buy request
options.description: The buy request creates the order and calls the payment gateway inline, returning only once the charge is confirmed, with no queue or worker tier.
pros: The buyer gets a definitive 'paid and confirmed' before the response returns — simplest UX.
No pending state, no reconciliation between reservation and payment outcome.
Fewer moving parts: no queue or worker tier to operate.
cons: The slow third-party payment call (hundreds of ms) holds the request open under peak load.
Payment-gateway latency or outages directly fail buys and pin server threads.
Throughput is capped by the slowest dependency in the chain, undermining the whole sale.

view.caption: The Purchase Service synchronously calls the Order Service, which persists the order to the Order Store and charges the Payment Service inline before returning.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why not create the order and charge the card synchronously inside the buy request?
whyNow: Pushing durable/slow work behind a queue keeps the contended path microsecond-cheap; it's why the design survives the spike.

concepts.definition: A time limit for an admitted winner to complete payment before the unit is released.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents non-payers from permanently locking scarce stock.
concepts.example: Reserve -> 10 min to pay -> else expire and release the unit.

recap.before: Heavy work on the contended path.
recap.after: Buy returns instantly; orders/payment processed async with a hold.
recap.newRisk: The fast counter and the durable ledger can drift; expired reservations need releasing.

failureDrills.scenario: A reserved buyer never completes payment.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The reservation expires after the hold window; the unit is released (counter incremented, ledger adjusted) and can resell.
failureDrills.mitigation: Reaper job on reservation TTL + counter/ledger reconciliation.

failureDrills.scenario: The order queue delivers the same reservation twice (at-least-once delivery or a worker crash after charging but before ack).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Exactly one order and one charge result; the duplicate is a no-op.
failureDrills.mitigation: Idempotent consumer: unique order per reservation_id, and a PSP idempotency key per reservation so a re-delivered message reuses the same payment_intent rather than double-charging.

failureDrills.scenario: Payment times out after the charge actually succeeded at the PSP (ambiguous outcome).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Don't blindly release — releasing a paid unit would oversell or refund-and-resell.
failureDrills.mitigation: Reconcile against the PSP (query payment_intent / wait for webhook) before releasing; the webhook is idempotent on payment_intent_id so the late 'succeeded' still confirms the order.

flows.name: Async order creation + payment
flows.note: A successful reservation is processed off the hot path: a worker creates the durable order, decrements the ledger, and charges the buyer within the hold window; failure releases the unit.

steps.title: 7. Consistency, Reconciliation, and Degradation
description: The in-memory counter is fast but not durable; the append-only stock_ledger_events table is the source of truth. The invariant: `remaining = total − reserves + releases`, equivalently `confirmed_orders + live_reservations ≤ total`. Reconcile periodically and rebuild the counter from the ledger after a restart (`remaining = total − Σ deltas`).

But reconciliation is only half the story — the live failure mode matters. When the Redis primary dies, fails over, or its authority is uncertain (replication lag, possible split-brain), the safe default is **fail closed for scarce inventory**: stop issuing 'reserved' responses and return 'try later / sold out', rather than risk a stale or split counter overselling. A new counter epoch is minted on rebuild and carried in the token/reservation as a fence, so writes from a deposed primary are rejected. Availability of the *buy* path is sacrificed before correctness — the no-oversell guarantee is never negotiable.

Released (expired) reservations append a `release` event and increment the counter back. A reaper enforces hold-window TTLs and is itself idempotent (keyed on reservation_id) so re-running it can't double-release a unit. Degrade gracefully elsewhere too: if a component is overwhelmed, fall back to shedding (clear 'sold out / try later'), keep the sold-out state correct, and accept that the CDN-cached availability flag may lag the true counter by its TTL — the authoritative buy path still rejects oversell even when stale 'in stock' is shown.

view.caption: A background job reconciles the in-memory Inventory Counter against the durable ledger in the Order Store, which the Purchase Service decrements and the Order Service persists to.

steps.decisionPrompt: The Redis counter node restarts mid-sale. How do you avoid overselling or losing stock?

concepts.definition: Keeping the fast in-memory counter consistent with the durable allocation ledger.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets you use a fast counter as the gate while the durable store stays authoritative.
concepts.example: On restart, remaining = total − Σ ledger deltas (reserve −1, release +1).

concepts.definition: When inventory truth is uncertain, stop granting reservations rather than risk overselling.
concepts.whyItMatters: Resolves counter-failover ambiguity: correctness (no oversell) outranks buy-path availability.
concepts.example: Redis primary failover ⇒ pause /buy, return 'try later' until the counter is rebuilt and fenced.

deepDives.title: Highly-available counter without overselling
points: Treat the counter as a fenced leader, not just a cache: each rebuild bumps an epoch; reservations and tokens carry it so a deposed primary's writes are rejected.
On primary failure, fail closed — pause buy admissions and serve 'try later' — until the counter is reconstructed from the ledger (`remaining = total − Σ deltas`).
Prefer a synchronously-replicated or quorum store for the counter so a failover can't lose acknowledged decrements; never trust an async replica that may have missed the last reserves.
For multi-SKU drops, partition the counter per SKU so one hot key's failure or contention is isolated.
Pre-minted reservation tokens (one per unit) are an alternative that makes oversell structurally impossible at the cost of a reclaim process — a good fallback when a single HA counter is too risky.

deepDives.title: Operating the drop: dashboards and kill switches
points: A flash sale is a live operation, not a fire-and-forget deploy — the operator needs real-time signals and manual controls for the few minutes it runs.

Dashboard signals: token-issuance rate, queue depth / estimated wait, reservation-success rate, payment-failure rate, hold-expiry rate, counter value vs. ledger total (reconciliation drift), and counter epoch / rebuild status.

Kill switches: pause/throttle admission (stop or slow token issuance), force sold-out (flip the cached flag + stop the buy path), trigger a counter rebuild from the ledger, and bump the epoch to fence a suspect primary.

These tie back to the admission feedback loop and fail-closed behavior: the same signals that drive automatic throttling are what the operator watches, and the same fail-closed path the system takes on counter uncertainty is also a button a human can press.

failureDrills.scenario: The Redis counter primary fails over mid-sale and the new primary's value is uncertain (replication lag / possible split-brain).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: No oversell. The buy path pauses ('try later') rather than trusting a possibly-stale counter.
failureDrills.mitigation: Fail closed; rebuild remaining from the ledger; bump the epoch and fence out the old primary before resuming.

failureDrills.scenario: CDN shows 'in stock' but the counter just hit zero.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Buyers may still attempt, but the authoritative buy path returns sold_out; no oversell.
failureDrills.mitigation: Short availability TTL to bound the staleness; treat the cached flag as advisory, the counter as truth.

whyNow: Closing on reconciliation + graceful degradation shows the fast-path design stays correct under failure — the no-oversell guarantee survives.

recap.before: Fast counter trusted blindly, even through failover.
recap.after: Counter reconciled to the durable ledger and fenced by epoch; on counter failure the buy path fails closed; failures shed cleanly; no oversell.
recap.newRisk: Reconciliation lag and reaper timing — bounded by conservative holds, idempotent reaping, and the durable ledger as authority.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Flash Sale
finalDesign.description: The CDN serves the static page and cached availability so the backend only sees transactional traffic. The edge enforces admission control (rate-limit, bot-filter, shed), and a virtual waiting room admits a fair, capped rate of users with signed tokens. Admitted buyers hit the purchase service, which verifies the token and performs a single atomic decrement-if-positive on an in-memory inventory counter — oversell-proof and lock-free. Success returns 'reserved' and enqueues the reservation; a worker creates the durable order, decrements the ledger, and charges the buyer's stored payment method within a hold window, releasing the unit on timeout. A reaper + reconciliation keep the fast counter consistent with the authoritative ledger, and the system degrades by shedding rather than overselling. Operationally, the drop is run from a live console: dashboards for token-issuance rate, queue depth, reservation-success and payment-failure rates, hold-expiry rate, counter value vs. ledger (reconciliation drift), and counter epoch/rebuild status; and kill switches for pausing admission, forcing sold-out, and triggering a counter rebuild — so an operator can throttle or fail closed in seconds.

view.caption: The buyer loads static pages from the CDN, enters via the Edge / API Gateway into the Virtual Waiting Room for a signed admission token, then buys through the Purchase Service which decrements the Inventory Counter and enqueues to the Order Queue for the Order Service to persist orders and charge payment, with the counter reconciled to the Order Store.

functional.how: Admission-gated buy path with an atomic stock counter.
Single-use token (max_qty) + idempotency-keyed reservation; unique (sale_id, user_id) caps per-user purchases.
Async order creation and payment within a hold.

nonFunctional.how: Guarded atomic decrement-if-positive; epoch-fenced counter that fails closed on failover; ledger reconciliation.
Virtual waiting room admits a capped, fair rate.
Shed cleanly with 'try later'; keep sold-out correct.
Rejections happen at the edge before the core.

technologyChoices.concern: Static offload + edge admission / waiting room
tradeoff: A managed CDN/edge absorbs the 10M-arrival read and waiting-room write burst that must never reach the purchase core.
Cloudflare/Fastly offer turnkey waiting-room products; self-hosting one means building durable, ordered, edge-replicated queue state yourself.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed waiting-room + WAF product removes most custom rate-limiter, bot-filter, and queue-position code at the edge.

tradeoff: A replicated log preserves entrant order and supports replay after an edge-region failure, but global FIFO across every shard is expensive.
A durable queue or KV store is enough when the fairness contract is best-effort FIFO within a shard/window and admissions are idempotent on queue_entry_id.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed waiting-room product with durable queue state, bot filtering, and regional failover removes most of the custom edge queue, position, and replay machinery.

tradeoff: Eligibility checks and bot challenges must run before token issue so fake accounts and scripted clients burn cheap edge resources instead of scarce reservations.
A strict risk gate protects fairness, but false positives need an operator override path because the sale window is short.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed edge security stack with WAF rules, bot challenges, identity signals, and per-account/device velocity limits reduces custom anti-abuse services for this interview.

tradeoff: Stateless signed tokens verify at the edge without a round-trip, but single-use consumption needs a small shared store (the admissions table / Redis) to mark them spent.
Pure JWT is cheapest; a managed identity platform adds account binding, MFA, and abuse signals for free.
Redis with a guarded Lua decrement is fastest but in-memory — it needs replication, fencing, and ledger rebuild for HA.
A DynamoDB/Cosmos conditional write (remaining > 0) is durable and HA out of the box, trading a little latency for no rebuild step.
AWS MemoryDB / Redis-with-quorum avoids losing acknowledged decrements on failover.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A durable conditional-write store (DynamoDB/Spanner/Cosmos) removes the separate counter-vs-ledger reconciliation entirely — the counter IS the durable truth.

tradeoff: At-least-once delivery from any of these requires an idempotent consumer (unique order per reservation_id) to avoid double orders/charges.
A managed queue removes broker operations; the burst is only ~hundreds/sec, so throughput isn't the constraint — durability and ordering are.
Append-only ledger events + unique constraints (sale_id+idempotency_key, one order per reservation) give correctness and the counter-rebuild source of truth.
A single SKU's order volume is tiny (≤ stock), so a plain relational DB is plenty — the value is constraints and durability, not scale.
Charge asynchronously with a PSP idempotency key per reservation so re-delivered queue messages or webhook retries don't double-charge.
Treat the webhook as the source of payment truth and reconcile ambiguous timeouts against the PSP before releasing a unit.

technologyChoices.concern: Hold expiry, reconciliation, and operator workflows
tradeoff: Timer-driven workflows make hold expiry, counter rebuilds, and reconciliation retries visible and restartable.
A simple cron/reaper is cheaper for one sale, but it hides state unless every attempt and operator action is persisted.
Workflow tooling does not replace the durable ledger; it coordinates work around the ledger.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A durable workflow service with timers, retries, and execution history removes much of the custom hold-expiry scheduler and manual replay console.

tradeoff: The key SLOs are operational and business-facing: token issue rate, queue depth, reservation success, payment failure, hold expiry, counter-vs-ledger drift, and counter epoch health.
Self-hosting Prometheus/Grafana gives custom labels and retention, but high-cardinality sale/user/device labels can become an operational problem during the burst.
Managed observability shortens setup for a one-time drop, but cost and retention controls need to be set before T0.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed observability suite with dashboards, alerts, traces, and audit logs removes much of the custom live-console telemetry plumbing.

say: Establish demand >> supply (100-1000x) and that most requests must fail cheaply.
Pin never-oversell, fairness, and graceful degradation as the hard goals.
Note the spike is at a known instant you design for.
Admission control + load shedding at the edge.
Virtual waiting room for fairness, gating with tokens.
Oversell-proof reservation via a single atomic counter.
Async order creation + payment with a reservation hold.
Reconciliation, reaping expired holds, and degradation.
Tradeoffs: how many to admit vs stock, counter durability, hold-window length.
Mention anti-bot/abuse and per-user purchase limits as extensions.

expectations: Uses a CDN and an atomic counter to avoid oversell.
May miss fairness and async order handling.
Reasons about counter/ledger reconciliation and restart safety.
Discusses fairness, anti-abuse, and how many to admit vs stock quantitatively.

followUps: How many users do you admit relative to stock, and why?
How do you defend against bots gaming the waiting room?
How would you enforce a per-user purchase limit under this concurrency?
How do you make the in-memory counter highly available without overselling?
How would you handle multiple SKUs / sizes in the same drop?
